title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read an article from which six sentences have been removed. Choose from the sentences A–G the one which fits each gap (37–42). There is one extra sentence which you do not need to use.

content.title: Seeing Red: The Psychology of Colour in Marketing
content.text: Walk into any supermarket and you are surrounded by a carefully engineered palette. The colours on packaging, signage and promotional displays are never accidental. Marketers have spent decades studying how colour influences mood, perception and, ultimately, purchasing decisions. (37) [37] La investigación muestra que el color puede ser el primer factor decisivo en una compra. [/37] Studies suggest that consumers make up their minds about a product within ninety seconds of first seeing it, and up to ninety per cent of that snap judgement is based on colour alone. || Red is perhaps the most powerful weapon in the marketer's arsenal. It raises the heart rate, creates a sense of urgency and is strongly associated with appetite stimulation. (38) [38] Por eso los restaurantes de comida rápida hacen un uso tan frecuente de este color. [/38] Fast-food chains from McDonald's to KFC have relied on red and yellow combinations for decades, a palette that research confirms triggers hunger and encourages quick turnover. || Blue, on the other hand, projects trustworthiness, calm and reliability. It is the dominant colour in banking, healthcare and technology, sectors where consumer confidence is paramount. (39) [39] Las empresas tecnológicas y financieras lo usan deliberadamente para transmitir seguridad. [/39] When PayPal, Facebook and Samsung all chose shades of blue for their branding, they were drawing on a vast body of psychological research supporting the colour's association with honesty. || Green carries connotations of nature, health and environmental responsibility. (40) [40] Las marcas ecológicas y orgánicas recurren casi siempre a esta gama cromática. [/40] Even companies with little genuine connection to sustainability have adopted green packaging in recent years, a practice that critics have labelled 'greenwashing'. Regulators in several countries are now scrutinising such claims more carefully. || The relationship between colour and culture complicates things further. White symbolises purity in Western markets but is associated with mourning in parts of East Asia. (41) [41] Un error en la elección del color puede dañar seriamente la imagen de una marca en un mercado extranjero. [/41] Several global brands have faced costly rebranding exercises after launching products without conducting adequate cultural colour research. || Ultimately, there is no universal formula. (42) [42] El contexto, la audiencia y el sector determinan qué colores resultan más eficaces. [/42] What works brilliantly in one category can be completely wrong in another. The most successful marketers understand that colour is a tool, not a guarantee.

paragraphs.A: The same logo redesigned in a different colour can generate entirely different emotional responses from consumers.
paragraphs.B: This explains why fast-food chains consistently use warm, stimulating tones in their branding.
paragraphs.C: Colour alone can determine whether a consumer picks up a product or walks past it.
paragraphs.D: Context, audience and industry sector all shape which colours prove most effective.
paragraphs.E: Organic and eco-friendly brands in particular lean heavily on green tones to signal their values.
paragraphs.F: This is why blue dominates the branding of banks, tech firms and health insurance providers.
paragraphs.G: A poor colour choice can alienate entire markets and require expensive corrections.

questions.explanation: C establece la importancia del color en la decisión de compra, enlazando directamente con la estadística del 90% que sigue.
B explica por qué las cadenas de comida rápida usan el rojo, conectando la idea de apetito con los ejemplos concretos de McDonald's y KFC.
F refuerza el uso deliberado del azul en sectores de confianza, preparando los ejemplos de PayPal, Facebook y Samsung.
E especifica qué tipo de marcas usan el verde, conectando con la crítica del 'greenwashing' que sigue.
A señala las consecuencias de elegir mal el color en mercados extranjeros, enlazando con los casos de rebranding costoso.
D introduce los factores que determinan la eficacia del color, cerrando el artículo con una reflexión matizada.
